main_summary.summary: Judge D'Army Bailey, interviewed by David Cline in Memphis on August 13, 2013, recounts a movement biography spanning Memphis's Crump-era Black politics, Southern University (Baton Rouge) student protests, Clark University in Worcester, Massachusetts, Yale Law School, the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council (LSCRRC), Berkeley California City Council, and the founding of the National Civil Rights Museum at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis. Bailey describes his 1961 expulsion from Southern under Rule 18 ("lack of university adjustment") after the Cox v. Louisiana protests, his role as LSCRRC National Director in 1967 placing law students with Southern civil rights attorneys, his 1971 Berkeley council election with the April Coalition and subsequent recall, and the 1982 foreclosure auction at which he bid $144,000 to save the Lorraine Motel. He details the political fight to secure $8.8 million in state, city, and county funds for the museum, his 1991 resignation from the board after Benjamin L. Hooks replaced him as president, and his critique of corporate capture of civil rights institutions.
key_themes: Foundation funding of civil rights infrastructure
Electoral politics and the April Coalition
Founding of the National Civil Rights Museum
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview provides a rare first-person account of the institutional and financial scaffolding behind the civil rights movement, particularly the role of LSCRRC, the Field Foundation under Leslie W. Dunbar, the Taconic Foundation, the Rockefeller Brothers Fund, and the Norman Foundation in sustaining Southern legal advocacy. Bailey is the primary witness to the December 13, 1982 foreclosure auction at which the Lorraine Motel was preserved for what became the National Civil Rights Museum, and his testimony about the 1991 board takeover by J.R. "Pitt" Hyde III and Benjamin L. Hooks documents a generational shift from movement-veteran leadership to corporate stewardship.

The interview also preserves the procedural mechanics of Cox v. Louisiana (1965) from a participant in the December 1961 Baton Rouge march that produced the case, and contextualizes Berkeley's 1971 April Coalition election (Bailey, Ira Simmons, Loni Hancock) within the post-Ron Dellums opening for Black-left electoral coalitions. Bailey's reflections on Lyndon B. Johnson, the limits of the Voting Rights Act, and the Hegelian dialectic of progress make this a substantive primary source on movement strategy.

chapters.title: The Difference Between an Activist and a Radical
chapters.summary: Opening the August 2013 interview with David Cline, Bailey draws the distinction that frames his autobiography: an activist works for change within accepted boundaries, while a radical bangs against the outer walls for far more than is institutionally conceived, fighting as if he will win even knowing he is lucky to reach the walls at all.

chapters.title: Why He Resists Labels
chapters.summary: He accepts the radical label the California media pinned on the Berkeley council takeover but explains why he avoids labels and even hesitated to join CORE in Baton Rouge: a definable group makes you an easier target for those who want to discount what you are trying to do.

chapters.title: The Movement Was Never One Leader
chapters.summary: Asked how he defines the civil rights movement, Bailey rejects the King-centered frame, recalling the museum's long arc from slavery and his own first-hand experience of the movement's intensity: sitting with Fannie Lou Hamer on her Ruleville porch, the SNCC Freedom Singers behind the museum's music exhibit, and mentors like Will D. Campbell, Anne and Carl Braden, and Bob Zellner met at a 1961 Wisconsin seminar.

chapters.title: Emmett Till and Little Rock
chapters.summary: As the first generation of his family to reach college, Bailey describes the movement happening accidentally in his freshman year, and traces his real awakening to seeing Emmett Till's body in Jet and watching the Little Rock students his own age, the reason the museum credits Brown v. Board as pivotal but treats Till's photograph as the spark that ignited Black America.

chapters.title: Riding the Front of the Bus
chapters.summary: He recounts riding the front section of the interstate bus from Memphis to Baton Rouge as the only Black passenger up front, whites refusing the seats behind him, and traveling alone through Georgia where he could easily have ended up missing, sustained by a refusal to let fear overcome his dignity even while shaking in his boots.

chapters.title: A Pullman Porter's Pass
chapters.summary: Bailey describes parents who married in high school without finishing, the entrepreneur grandfather he was named for, his mother the respected housekeeper and receptionist at Dr. Seagerson's clinic, and his father who rose from mail handler to porter on the Illinois Central's New Orleans-to-Chicago line, the railroad pass letting the family travel free to ballgames in St. Louis and Chicago.

chapters.title: Raised to Be Independent
chapters.summary: He recalls the latitude his mother gave him and his brother Walter, her own path from a GED to one of Memphis's first Black female barbers to a licensed practical nurse at St. Joseph's, parents who voted but did not organize, a cautious father, the segregated train station, and the calm both parents showed when he was expelled from Southern.

chapters.title: The Crump Machine and the Black Vote
chapters.summary: Bailey explains that Memphis Blacks could vote early because Boss Crump's machine, which controlled the city and much of Tennessee, drew strength from a Black vote delivered through lieutenants who could broker jobs and favors, with the cautious NAACP the only real civil rights organization as the machine declined in the mid-1950s.

chapters.title: The Shelby County Democratic Club
chapters.summary: He describes working the sound trucks as a teenager for the Shelby County Democratic Club, the strongest Black political organization of his youth, supporting white moderates and trailblazing Black candidates like J.E. Walker and Russell Sugarmon, whose near-win drove the white community to collapse its field to stop him, an early lesson in the inseparability of politics and race.

chapters.title: No Youth Wing, and the Bootlegger Job
chapters.summary: With no NAACP youth wing, Bailey and his brother sat among seasoned strategists rather than apart from them, and he recounts the summer the Democratic Club paid him through the sheriff's office to hunt bootleggers, delivering a half-pint of corn whiskey when he collected his check, the give-and-take that got things done.

chapters.title: Freshman Class President
chapters.summary: Elected freshman class president at Southern in 1959 with no civil rights agenda, Bailey describes an early awakening to a massacre of Black activists in South Africa, his campus newspaper column, and bringing rhythm-and-blues acts like Gene Bowlegs Miller's band to Baton Rouge, a student leader enjoying Louisiana before the movement reached him.

chapters.title: Greensboro Reaches Southern
chapters.summary: A month or two after Greensboro, Southern students sat in at downtown Baton Rouge lunch counters and were arrested, then expelled by their own administration; their case became Garner v. Louisiana, the 1961 Supreme Court decision that one cannot be convicted of breach of peace for peaceful conduct that merely provokes others to react.

chapters.title: Tear Gas and Cox v. Louisiana
chapters.summary: Bailey recounts the December 1961 march into downtown Baton Rouge met with tear gas and dogs, standing at the head of the line with his brother beside Reverend B. Elton Cox, whose arrest produced Cox v. Louisiana; the divided Court reversed the conviction by reasoning the police had given permission, dancing around the courthouse-demonstration statute it would not strike down.

chapters.title: The National Student Association
chapters.summary: At the 1960 National Student Association convention at the University of Minnesota, Bailey watched a national debate over endorsing the sit-ins and met Tom Hayden, Allard Lowenstein, and Barney Frank; he saw a movement of white and Black students alike, an NSA with a Washington lobbyist and a CIA-tainted international wing, against the backdrop of the HUAC fight and a film branding the activists communist.

chapters.title: Port Huron and the Boycott
chapters.summary: He keeps an original copy of the 1962 Port Huron Statement that founded Students for a Democratic Society, whose people he knew, and describes how, after the Cox arrest in December 1961, protest broke out again at Southern and the university president closed the school and ordered students home for the holidays.

chapters.title: Rule 18 and a School Shut Down
chapters.summary: Warned he would be expelled if he spoke, Bailey spoke and was severed under Rule 18, a catch-all titled lack of university adjustment also used against gay students, while President Felton Clark's campus assembly ordered every student off by five o'clock; three of the expelled took a taxi back to register and were driven off campus ahead of the state troopers, the protest crushed.

chapters.title: Nonviolence as Strategy
chapters.summary: Bailey frames nonviolence as a strategic necessity rather than a belief: with three thousand marchers facing dogs and tear gas and nowhere to fight back, there was no other choice, and they stayed well-dressed and orderly even as they ran from the gas.

chapters.title: Dion Diamond and the SNCC Circle
chapters.summary: With the CORE national office paying their hotel bill, Bailey describes SNCC organizer Dion Diamond jailed at Southern and field secretaries Chuck McDew and Bob Zellner arrested for criminal anarchy when they came to visit, an involvement that drew Bailey to SNCC staff meetings in Atlanta where he came to know John Lewis, Julian Bond, and director Jim Forman.

chapters.title: Northern Student Movement, and a Lawsuit
chapters.summary: Wary of being pinned to any one group, Bailey formed his own Student Action Committee, then connected with Peter Countryman's Northern Student Movement at Yale and started a Worcester chapter; the CORE-backed lawsuit by the New Orleans firm Collins, Douglas and Elie to reverse his Southern expulsion settled by readmitting everyone except him and Ronnie Moore.

chapters.title: A Scholarship to Clark
chapters.summary: A referral from Walter Williams, a Jackson State student expelled like him, brought a scholarship to Clark University in Worcester, raised by students through car washes and bake sales; Bailey took the SAT at what is now Rhodes College and was pulled aside to lunch separately in 1962, the Clark effort noted in the Senate by Jacob Javits.

chapters.title: A Different World in Worcester
chapters.summary: Bailey describes the long railroad route north on his father's pass through upstate New York to Worcester, rooming with Fred Jealous, father of future NAACP head Ben Jealous, and the fifth of Jack Daniels two friendly students gave him for his twenty-first birthday in an unfamiliar white world.

chapters.title: Wyman-Gordon and the John Birch Owner
chapters.summary: Tiring of being asked only about the South, Bailey turned the question on Worcester's own segregation and took on Wyman-Gordon, run by John Birch Society national secretary Robert Stoddard and employing eighteen Black workers, all janitors; with Abbie Hoffman attached as publicist through his newsletter The Drum, the group picketed and brought the plant to a stop.

chapters.title: Forcing a Corporation to Change
chapters.summary: Bailey describes Stoddard's grip on the Worcester newspaper, WTAG radio, and the Clark board, and how a multifaceted strategy, the picket plus complaints to the Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination and the Defense Department, forced the corporation to change, proving a small force with the right research can move a giant; a one-is-enough editorial drew a page of answering letters, and Clark later gave him an honorary doctorate.

chapters.title: The Police and the Trucks
chapters.summary: He recalls little fear in Worcester, student counter-picketers, and a police sergeant who disliked the protest but needed the driveway cleared for trucks; Bailey left the choice to the students and they relented at activist priest Father Bernard Gilgun's plea, the standoff as much a union issue as a civil rights one.

chapters.title: Refined Northern Opposition
chapters.summary: Bailey contrasts the refined, quiet opposition of the Northeast with Southern threats, picketing the Worcester Area Council of Churches after it refused him a hearing and answering a local NAACP head's charge of recklessness by packing the chapter with students to take a position of strength from within.

chapters.title: Bringing Malcolm X to Worcester
chapters.summary: Bailey recounts calling Malcolm X at his New York home to invite him to Clark, board ministers demanding an opposing voice, and Malcolm offering to debate Dr. King but refusing the substitute Wyatt Tee Walker; he spent the day with Malcolm at a WTAG interview, agreeing with much of what he said while declining, as ever, nonviolence as a creed.

chapters.title: The Deacons for Defense
chapters.summary: Crossing the Lake Pontchartrain bridge to a Bogalusa protest in the summer of 1965, Bailey was met and escorted by the armed Deacons for Defense and Justice, the shotguns alongside the march reassuring rather than troubling him; he draws his own line between such self-defense and the proactive violence of groups like the Weathermen.

chapters.title: Title VII at Crown Zellerbach
chapters.summary: Working that summer from Ernest Dutch Morial's New Orleans office, Bailey was one of the law students the NAACP Legal Defense Fund sent to file the first Title VII complaints after the provision took effect in 1965, getting Crown Zellerbach workers in Bogalusa to file some of the earliest and most effective complaints under the new law.

chapters.title: The Law Students Civil Rights Research Council
chapters.summary: Bailey describes his first law year at Boston University and the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council, funded by the Taconic Foundation, Rockefeller Brothers Fund, and others to send top law students South for thirty dollars a week to assist overwhelmed movement lawyers, its headquarters housed in the national ACLU office.

chapters.title: A Stop at Yale That Changed Everything
chapters.summary: Rejected by Yale, Columbia, and Harvard, Bailey stopped by chance at Yale on a drive back to Clark and met Dean of Admissions Jack Tate, a man from near Memphis who took to him; the waitlist Tate arranged became a transfer admission the next summer, an accident of stopping by that redirected his life.

chapters.title: National Director, and the Draft
chapters.summary: Elected national director of the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council from the ACLU's New York office, Bailey won a draft deferment from his Memphis board as work in the national interest; convinced the war was wrong, he believed he would rather have gone to jail than to Vietnam, his conviction sharpened by Howard Zinn and Norman Thomas at the teach-ins.

chapters.title: The Night King Was Killed
chapters.summary: Bailey recounts raising grant money to send ten law students to the Memphis sanitation strike, one of them, Alex Hurder, briefly the first suspect in King's assassination as a white man near the Lorraine Motel, and the night his secretary brought word from the radio that King had been shot, then a minute later that he was dead.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: A New Life in San Francisco
chapters.summary: With his Law Students Council year ending in 1968, Bailey turned down a Cummins Engine Foundation offer from Philip Sorensen for a San Francisco legal-services job arranged by Steve Antler, taking two months first to travel Europe on five dollars a day before beginning test-case practice.

chapters.title: Out on a Limb
chapters.summary: Doing central-office consumer-fraud litigation, Bailey admits he is not built to work under others; he challenged the leadership over unfair treatment of staff, told the board the director should be fired, and was fired himself, then sued with a white colleague's help, taking from it the lesson that those who agree with you quietly will leave you out on the limb alone.

chapters.title: Money Makes Change
chapters.topic: The Untold Story of Foundation Money
chapters.summary: A guilt-ridden young friend with inherited wealth asked Bailey what to do with it, and Bailey answered, give me a million dollars; his conviction that change takes money frames the untold story of foundation money sustaining the movement, the foundations Congress later intimidated, and Leslie Dunbar of the Field Foundation backing Marian Wright Edelman and choosing John Lewis over Bailey for the Voter Education Project.

chapters.title: The Foundations That Funded the Movement
chapters.summary: Consulting for the Field Foundation under chair Ruth Field, Bailey got five thousand dollars from Dunbar after two students were killed at Southern University in 1972 to convene a Black people's community inquiry with John Lewis, Owusu Sadaukai, and W. Haywood Burns, and describes the consortium of foundations, Norman, Taconic, Rockefeller Brothers, New York, Cummins, Stern, that could pool money for movement work from Bailey's projects to Cesar Chavez's farm workers.

chapters.title: The Bread Limited Trust
chapters.summary: The friend countered with a million-dollar trust at Bank of America whose dividends Bailey could give away freely, with a salary and access to the corpus, mandated to fund the non-tax-exempt projects the foundations would not touch; he named it the Bread Limited Trust, the independent income that floated him after the legal-services firing as he moved into Berkeley politics.

chapters.title: Deciding to Run
chapters.summary: Ron Dellums's election to Congress opened a Berkeley council vacancy, and over an evening at Ira Simmons's home the two decided to run; the white left's April Coalition had a deal to split a slate with the Berkeley Black Caucus, and both the caucus and Dellums pressed the newcomers not to run.

chapters.title: The Black Community First
chapters.summary: Refusing to drop his running mate, Bailey forced the caucus to accept them both and got on the slate with Loni Hancock, registering to vote only when he signed up as a candidate, having never attended a council meeting; he announced his first priority as the interest of the Black community, a pure Southern instinct carried to Northern California.

chapters.title: A Radical Takeover
chapters.summary: Bailey opened a separate campaign office in the Black community rather than running joint with the white candidates, establishing legitimacy with a suspicious base before winning two of three seats with Hancock; Julian Bond cut him a radio spot, a strong campaign manager steadied the effort, and the coalition held despite his insistence on his own agenda, the press dubbing it a radical takeover.

chapters.title: The Panthers and the Recall Threat
chapters.summary: Bailey met Huey Newton at his penthouse and supported Panther programs like free breakfast and daycare without joining or giving the twenty-five thousand dollars Newton asked for; the conservatives' plan to recall all three council members narrowed when Black councilman Wilmot Sweeney objected to sparing the white woman, then focused on Bailey alone.

chapters.part: The Recall and the Money Question
chapters.title: Mike Wallace and 60 Minutes
chapters.summary: Bailey describes filibustering council meetings until the Black mayor cut his microphone and he stood to keep talking, the twenty-five thousand dollars he listed as anonymous fueling a carpetbagger charge, and Mike Wallace turning from charming to hostile the moment the 60 Minutes camera rolled, demanding to know where his money came from while Bailey refused to say.

chapters.part: The Recall and the Money Question
chapters.title: The Spy and the Money Question
chapters.summary: Warned by Wallace that a funded group meant to expose his backers, Bailey learned from Dunbar it was the Fund for Investigative Journalism on a Stern family grant; a self-revealed government spy who had infiltrated his campaign and later wrote The Glass House Tapes falsely claimed on Los Angeles radio that he had paid Bailey ten thousand dollars to infiltrate the Angela Davis defense, a charge KPFA declined to air.

chapters.part: The Recall and the Money Question
chapters.title: How a Recall Works
chapters.summary: After an extortion plot that masqueraded as a contract on his life, Bailey explains the mechanics of recall, twenty-five percent of the last election's voters forcing a yes-or-no ballot, and the bind of needing a majority to survive when he had only ever won a plurality, with ACLU founder Roger Baldwin warning he could not beat it without revealing his money.

chapters.part: The Recall and the Money Question
chapters.title: The Money Runs Out
chapters.summary: As the questions mounted, Bailey's friends closed the trust, leaving him a hundred thousand dollars they paid taxes on; a staff of thirteen, several on work-study, armed him with thick research books that drove change at the council, but most of what remained went to fighting the recall, the money running out regardless after two and a half years.

chapters.title: What Lasted in Berkeley
chapters.summary: Bailey points to the integrated city hall and the police and fire opportunities that outlasted him, and recounts breaking a garbage workers' strike by taking the workers aside to show them they were carrying the other municipal unions on their backs, then winning them an accelerated raise to bring them level with the rest.

chapters.title: "We Shall Overcome"
chapters.summary: After noting the gentrification that later shrank Berkeley's Black population, Bailey turns to the Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts, recalling Lyndon Johnson putting the weight of the presidency behind them and saying we shall overcome after Selma and the deaths of Viola Liuzzo and James Reeb, even as Bailey knew from Memphis that the vote without money or power changes little.

chapters.title: Voting Is Only a Tool
chapters.summary: Bailey insists he ran for office as opportunity, not belief in the system the media said radicals had joined, and that the riots in Watts, Detroit, and Newark were what truly forced change; strategies must shift before they are neutralized, and a political system designed to be controlled by money, with unions decimated and no labor voice to offset it, will not itself deliver liberation.

chapters.title: The Judge and the Death-Penalty Lawyer
chapters.summary: Nineteen years a trial judge and fourteen a lawyer who tried more than a dozen capital cases, Bailey describes the working truth that lawyers seek victory rather than justice and that the legal system orders disputes short of violence rather than delivers them; he recalls William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy at the 1991 museum opening urging activists toward the state courts as the federal courts closed.

chapters.title: Citizens United and Bush v. Gore
chapters.summary: Bailey describes a federal judiciary grown unsympathetic, Citizens United handing corporations unlimited electoral money, Bush v. Gore as a judicial coup against the majority, and Justice Antonin Scalia telling the public to get over it, leaving activists to ask where they can now turn to fight.

chapters.title: Hegel's Dialectic
chapters.summary: Watching an educational and material decline for a generation no longer sure of out-pacing its parents, Bailey reaches for Hegel's dialectic as his frame, thesis and antithesis and synthesis, oppression and rebellion and partial progress cycling on, the Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts real landmarks but never the game-changers people hoped, Title VII never ending employment racism.

chapters.title: The Pathology This Country Created
chapters.summary: Bailey reads the explosion of Black imprisonment as a symptom of a country that destroyed minority self-worth across three centuries, impossible to erase in fifty years, and points to the psychological toll of everyday racism documented even in a Columbia University study of its effect on Black health.

chapters.title: The Cycle of Prison
chapters.summary: Bailey describes the pathology that sends people through prison and back unchanged, the senseless gang violence that gives the young a sense of bigness and worth, and a society that condemns the end result while denying its complicity; reversal, he argues, takes resources and a different attitude, and the claim of not seeing color is simply a lie.

chapters.title: Beyond Race: The Obama Election
chapters.summary: Bailey calls the beyond-race talk around the Obama election nonsense, recalling that he voted early and spent election night on a Gulf cruise, glad to be spared the exhilaration ashore because he knew the same battles would be waiting the next morning, only now with people patting themselves on the back.

chapters.part: Coming Home, and the Lorraine Motel
chapters.title: Return to Memphis
chapters.summary: Bailey describes returning to Memphis in 1974 to practice law beside his brother, writing a weekly newspaper column for seven years to soften his California-radical image, running for mayor in 1983 as a platform for his ideas, and being elected judge in 1990, and back in 1982 running into Walter Bailey.

chapters.part: Coming Home, and the Lorraine Motel
chapters.title: Walter Bailey and the Lorraine Motel
chapters.summary: Bailey recounts Walter Bailey, the Lorraine Motel's owner who happened to share a name with his father, struggling to keep the doors open after his wife died days after the assassination; the motel had decayed to a seedy strip with prostitutes in its rooms, save the small hallowed shrine Bailey kept of King's last room, his dishes, and his wife's effects, open to the public for donations.

chapters.part: Coming Home, and the Lorraine Motel
chapters.title: The Foreclosure
chapters.summary: Bailey incorporated the Lorraine Motel King Shrine Foundation that drew no support, watched Bailey carry his mortgage payment in a paper bag to the lender, and failed to assemble private buyers through a local contractor; the foreclosure and Bailey's bankruptcy stalled it until WDIA radio launched a campaign to save the motel and called Bailey in to help.

chapters.title: The Auction Begins
chapters.summary: At the December 13, 1982 foreclosure auction on a roughly 153,000-dollar mortgage, the mortgage holder's son bid for the lender while Bailey arrived with about fifty thousand dollars raised through radiothons, until the union's Jim Smith appeared thirty minutes early with a twenty-five-thousand-dollar check.

chapters.title: Bidding to $144,000
chapters.summary: On the courthouse steps A.W. Willis proposed a fifty-thousand-dollar loan from the Black-owned Tri-State Bank, whose head Jesse Turner was in Bailey's group, backed by last-minute guarantees from Jim Smith and cosmetics man Paul Shapiro; Bailey bid the lender up to a hundred forty-four thousand dollars and called a halt, and the hammer fell.

chapters.title: A Bank Man Now
chapters.summary: Bailey called Peabody Hotel owner Jack Belz that night to raise the final four thousand dollars for the cashier's check and suddenly owned a museum with no plan, letting Walter Bailey keep running the place; a threat from Coretta Scott King's lawyer to sue over the King name led him to drop it and rename the group the Lorraine Motel Civil Rights Museum Foundation, a blessing in disguise.

chapters.title: A Place to Make New Activists
chapters.summary: Freed by the name change from clearing every decision with a King family that called the project morbid, Bailey describes a vision drawn from his own movement experience: not a memorial to King but a place of magnetism that would rekindle the movement's spirit and convert the young into new activists, with volunteer architects drafting the first rough plans.

chapters.title: An $8.8 Million Dream
chapters.summary: A fifty-thousand-dollar grant raised through a Peabody gathering funded consultant proposals, and Bailey chose Benjamin Lawless, whose design captured the spirit he wanted: dynamic, immersive exhibits, a burned bus, the tear gas and barking dogs meant to put visitors on the scene, at a price tag of 8.8 million dollars.

chapters.title: Lobbying the Legislature
chapters.summary: Lawless's formula called for half from the state and a quarter each from city and county; after the county refused and a legislative bill stalled, Bailey called Governor Lamar Alexander to find it, then lobbied Nashville office to office, presenting the museum as a Martin Luther King project to a wary Democratic legislature that feared it would stir antagonism.

chapters.title: The Black Legislators' Priority
chapters.summary: A white legislator told Bailey frankly that the Black Memphis legislators had to make the museum their number-one priority over their own projects, and when they did the bill passed the House 83 to 17 and the Senate 33 to nothing; the funding settled at 2.2 million each from city and county and 4.4 million from the state, with Paul Shapiro's ten thousand dollars the only local business gift.

chapters.title: Giving Title to the State
chapters.summary: With A.W. Willis falling to cancer and the three governments refusing to hand money to a nonprofit, Bailey deeded the property to the state on condition it build the museum to Lawless's plans, glad to limit local political interference; an architect fight was resolved by giving the job to the Nashville Black firm McKissack and McKissack under the state architect's control.

chapters.part: Losing the Museum, and What It Means
chapters.title: Keeping the Board at Bay
chapters.summary: As construction raced ahead, Bailey stonewalled and filibustered his own board to keep their politics out of the exhibit design, while the board pushed new elections and bylaw changes to gain control and a millionaire member, J.R. Pitt Hyde, stepped into the vacuum and aligned with the faction against him.

chapters.part: Losing the Museum, and What It Means
chapters.title: Better to Ask Forgiveness
chapters.summary: Bailey raced to build the museum right before the board could seize control, on the principle that it is better to ask forgiveness than permission, while the opposition recruited Benjamin Hooks, a longtime absentee board member, to stand for president against him.

chapters.part: Losing the Museum, and What It Means
chapters.title: Resignation
chapters.summary: A nine-to-six vote installed Hooks as president and Hyde as executive-committee chairman, and Bailey read a resignation letter he had already prepared; his plan, marked by bringing Bob Moses, Julian Bond, and John Lewis to the opening, had been to make the site a center of action rather than the corporate institution it became.

chapters.part: Losing the Museum, and What It Means
chapters.title: Center, Not Museum
chapters.topic: What He Wanted It to Be
chapters.summary: Bailey explains naming it a museum rather than a center for political safety while he still needed government approvals, his real aim being exhibits that would re-energize visitors into activism; he voices his disappointment that it never became that, and the board's answer that if he wanted such a thing he should build his own.

chapters.part: Losing the Museum, and What It Means
chapters.title: Captured by the Corporations
chapters.summary: Bailey names the corporations seated at the board, FedEx, International Paper, AutoZone under Pitt Hyde during a discrimination suit, First Tennessee, and charges that corporate funding has killed the movement from the museum to an NAACP that will not cross its fast-food donors, closing on his creed that institutions serve only as long as they make change and become obstacles when they do not.

loc_video.caption: D'Army Bailey oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Memphis, Tennessee, 2013 August 13
